---
title: "IS4010: AI-Enhanced Application Development"
subtitle: "Week 12: Generics and Traits"
author: "Brandon M. Greenwell"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: dracula
    transition: fade
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
    preview-links: auto
    footer: "IS4010: App Development with AI"
    scrollable: true
    css: custom.css
---

## Week 12 Overview {.inverse}

**Session 1: Generic Types**

- Understanding the problem: code duplication
- Generic functions and structs
- Generic enums (Option, Result)
- Generic methods and implementations
- Introduction to trait bounds

**Session 2: Traits Deep Dive**

- Trait definitions and implementations
- Default implementations and trait parameters
- Trait bounds and where clauses
- Common traits: Debug, Clone, Display, Iterator
- Implementing traits for custom types

::: {.notes}
This week focuses on Rust's powerful abstraction mechanisms. Generics enable code reuse without runtime cost. Traits define shared behavior and enable polymorphism without inheritance.
:::

---

## Why Generics and Traits Matter {.inverse}

**Real-world impact:**

- **Rust standard library**: Built on generics (`Vec<T>`, `Option<T>`, `Result<T, E>`)
- **Code reuse**: Write once, use with any type
- **Type safety**: Compile-time guarantees without runtime cost
- **Zero-cost abstractions**: No performance penalty

**Industry examples:**

- [Servo](https://servo.org/): Mozilla's parallel browser engine
- [Tokio](https://tokio.rs/): Async runtime using generic futures
- [Serde](https://serde.rs/): Generic serialization framework

::: {.notes}
Generics are foundational to Rust's design philosophy. They enable the standard library's power while maintaining performance.
:::

---

# Session 1: Generics and Traits

## Understanding the Problem {.smaller}

**Without generics, you need duplicate code:**

```rust
fn largest_i32(list: &[i32]) -> &i32 {
    let mut largest = &list[0];
    for item in list {
        if item > largest {
            largest = item;
        }
    }
    largest
}

fn largest_f64(list: &[f64]) -> &f64 {
    let mut largest = &list[0];
    for item in list {
        if item > largest {
            largest = item;
        }
    }
    largest
}
```

**Problem**: Same logic, different types → code duplication!

::: {.notes}
This motivates generics. Ask students: "What happens when you need largest_u32, largest_char, etc.?"
:::

---

## Introducing Generics {.smaller}

**Generic functions work with multiple types:**

```rust
fn largest<T: PartialOrd>(list: &[T]) -> &T {
    let mut largest = &list[0];
    for item in list {
        if item > largest {
            largest = item;
        }
    }
    largest
}

// Works with any type that can be compared!
let numbers = vec![34, 50, 25, 100, 65];
let result = largest(&numbers);

let chars = vec!['y', 'm', 'a', 'q'];
let result = largest(&chars);
```

**Key concept**: `T: PartialOrd` is a [trait bound](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch10-02-traits.html#trait-bounds) - T must implement PartialOrd.

[📖 Rust Book: Generic Data Types](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch10-01-syntax.html)

::: {.notes}
Emphasize that generics are checked at compile time. No runtime cost!
:::

---

## Generic Structs {.smaller}

**Define structs that work with any type:**

```rust
struct Point<T> {
    x: T,
    y: T,
}

fn main() {
    let integer_point = Point { x: 5, y: 10 };
    let float_point = Point { x: 1.0, y: 4.0 };
}
```

**Multiple type parameters:**

```rust
struct Point<T, U> {
    x: T,
    y: U,
}

fn main() {
    let mixed = Point { x: 5, y: 4.0 };  // T=i32, U=f64
}
```

[📖 Rust Book: Generic Structs](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch10-01-syntax.html#in-struct-definitions)

::: {.notes}
Show that each type parameter can be different. This is more flexible than requiring all fields to be the same type.
:::

---

## Generic Enums {.smaller}

**You've been using generic enums all along:**

```rust
// Option<T> from standard library
enum Option<T> {
    Some(T),
    None,
}

// Result<T, E> for error handling
enum Result<T, E> {
    Ok(T),
    Err(E),
}
```

**Usage:**

```rust
fn divide(a: f64, b: f64) -> Result<f64, String> {
    if b == 0.0 {
        Err(String::from("Division by zero"))
    } else {
        Ok(a / b)
    }
}
```

[📖 Rust Book: Generic Enums](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch10-01-syntax.html#in-enum-definitions)

::: {.notes}
Connect to previous week - they've already used generic enums without realizing it!
:::

---

## Generic Methods {.smaller}

**Methods on generic structs:**

```rust
struct Point<T> {
    x: T,
    y: T,
}

impl<T> Point<T> {
    fn x(&self) -> &T {
        &self.x
    }
}

// Method only for specific type
impl Point<f32> {
    fn distance_from_origin(&self) -> f32 {
        (self.x.powi(2) + self.y.powi(2)).sqrt()
    }
}
```

**Notice**: `impl<T>` declares the generic, then `Point<T>` uses it.

[📖 Rust Book: Generic Methods](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch10-01-syntax.html#in-method-definitions)

::: {.notes}
Show that you can implement methods for all T, or only for specific types.
:::

---

## What Are Traits? {.smaller}

**Traits define shared behavior:**

```rust
pub trait Summary {
    fn summarize(&self) -> String;
}

struct NewsArticle {
    headline: String,
    content: String,
}

impl Summary for NewsArticle {
    fn summarize(&self) -> String {
        format!("{}: {}", self.headline, self.content)
    }
}
```

**Think of traits as:**
- Interfaces (Java/C#)
- Protocols (Swift)
- Type classes (Haskell)

[📖 Rust Book: Traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch10-02-traits.html)

::: {.notes}
Traits are Rust's way of defining shared behavior without inheritance.
:::

---

## Default Trait Implementations {.smaller}

**Traits can provide default behavior:**

```rust
pub trait Summary {
    fn summarize_author(&self) -> String;

    fn summarize(&self) -> String {
        format!("(Read more from {}...)", self.summarize_author())
    }
}

struct Tweet {
    username: String,
    content: String,
}

impl Summary for Tweet {
    fn summarize_author(&self) -> String {
        format!("@{}", self.username)
    }
    // summarize() uses the default implementation
}
```

[📖 Rust Book: Default Implementations](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch10-02-traits.html#default-implementations)

::: {.notes}
Default implementations reduce boilerplate. Types can override or use the default.
:::

---

## Traits as Parameters {.smaller}

**Accept any type implementing a trait:**

```rust
pub fn notify(item: &impl Summary) {
    println!("Breaking news! {}", item.summarize());
}

// Longer syntax (trait bound):
pub fn notify<T: Summary>(item: &T) {
    println!("Breaking news! {}", item.summarize());
}

// Multiple trait bounds:
pub fn notify<T: Summary + Display>(item: &T) {
    // Can call summarize() and also use {} formatting
}
```

[📖 Rust Book: Traits as Parameters](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch10-02-traits.html#traits-as-parameters)

::: {.notes}
`impl Trait` is syntactic sugar for trait bounds. Use whichever is clearer.
:::

---

## The `where` Clause {.smaller}

**Make complex trait bounds readable:**

```rust
// Hard to read:
fn some_function<T: Display + Clone, U: Clone + Debug>(t: &T, u: &U) -> i32 {
    // ...
}

// Much clearer with where clause:
fn some_function<T, U>(t: &T, u: &U) -> i32
where
    T: Display + Clone,
    U: Clone + Debug,
{
    // ...
}
```

**Use `where` when:**
- Multiple trait bounds
- Complex generic relationships
- Improves readability

[📖 Rust Book: where Clauses](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch10-02-traits.html#clearer-trait-bounds-with-where-clauses)

::: {.notes}
Where clauses are especially helpful with associated types and lifetime bounds.
:::

---

## Returning Trait Types {.smaller}

**Return types that implement traits:**

```rust
fn returns_summarizable() -> impl Summary {
    Tweet {
        username: String::from("rustacean"),
        content: String::from("Rust is awesome!"),
    }
}
```

**Limitation**: Can only return ONE concrete type:

```rust
// ❌ ERROR: Can't return different types
fn returns_summarizable(switch: bool) -> impl Summary {
    if switch {
        NewsArticle { /* ... */ }
    } else {
        Tweet { /* ... */ }  // Error!
    }
}
```

[📖 Rust Book: Returning Traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch10-02-traits.html#returning-types-that-implement-traits)

::: {.notes}
For returning different types, you need trait objects (Box<dyn Trait>) - advanced topic.
:::

---

## Common Standard Library Traits {.smaller}

**Frequently used traits you should know:**

- **[`Debug`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/fmt/trait.Debug.html)**: Format with `{:?}` (derive with `#[derive(Debug)]`)
- **[`Clone`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/clone/trait.Clone.html)**: Create deep copies with `.clone()`
- **[`Copy`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/marker/trait.Copy.html)**: Types that can be copied by just copying bits
- **[`Display`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/fmt/trait.Display.html)**: Format with `{}` (implement manually)
- **[`PartialEq`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/cmp/trait.PartialEq.html)**: Compare with `==` and `!=`
- **[`Eq`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/cmp/trait.Eq.html)**: Full equivalence relation
- **[`PartialOrd`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/cmp/trait.PartialOrd.html)**: Compare with `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`
- **[`Ord`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/cmp/trait.Ord.html)**: Total ordering
- **[`Iterator`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/iter/trait.Iterator.html)**: Types that can be iterated over

[📖 Rust Standard Library Traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/#traits)

::: {.notes}
These traits are fundamental. Most can be automatically derived with #[derive(...)].
:::

---

## Deriving Traits {.smaller}

**Automatically implement common traits:**

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
struct Point {
    x: i32,
    y: i32,
}

fn main() {
    let p1 = Point { x: 1, y: 2 };
    let p2 = p1.clone();

    println!("{:?}", p1);  // Debug
    assert_eq!(p1, p2);    // PartialEq
}
```

**Can derive:**
- `Debug`, `Clone`, `Copy`
- `PartialEq`, `Eq`, `PartialOrd`, `Ord`
- `Hash`, `Default`

**Cannot derive**: `Display`, `Iterator` (must implement manually)

[📖 Rust Book: Derivable Traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/appendix-03-derivable-traits.html)

::: {.notes}
Derive whenever possible - it's less error-prone than manual implementation.
:::

---

## Implementing Display Trait {.smaller}

**Custom formatting for user-facing output:**

```rust
use std::fmt;

struct Point {
    x: i32,
    y: i32,
}

impl fmt::Display for Point {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter) -> fmt::Result {
        write!(f, "({}, {})", self.x, self.y)
    }
}

fn main() {
    let p = Point { x: 1, y: 2 };
    println!("Point: {}", p);  // Point: (1, 2)
}
```

[📖 Rust Book: Display Trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/fmt/trait.Display.html)

::: {.notes}
Display is for user-facing output. Debug is for programmer-facing output.
:::

---

## Implementing Iterator Trait {.smaller}

**Make your types iterable:**

```rust
struct Counter {
    count: u32,
}

impl Counter {
    fn new() -> Counter {
        Counter { count: 0 }
    }
}

impl Iterator for Counter {
    type Item = u32;  // Associated type

    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item> {
        if self.count < 5 {
            self.count += 1;
            Some(self.count)
        } else {
            None
        }
    }
}
```

[📖 Rust Book: Iterator Trait](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch13-02-iterators.html)

::: {.notes}
Iterator is powerful - once implemented, you get all iterator methods for free!
:::

---

## Generic Stack Example {.smaller}

**Building a generic data structure:**

```rust
struct Stack<T> {
    items: Vec<T>,
}

impl<T> Stack<T> {
    fn new() -> Stack<T> {
        Stack { items: Vec::new() }
    }

    fn push(&mut self, item: T) {
        self.items.push(item);
    }

    fn pop(&mut self) -> Option<T> {
        self.items.pop()
    }

    fn is_empty(&self) -> bool {
        self.items.is_empty()
    }
}
```

[🎮 Try it in Playground](https://play.rust-lang.org/)

::: {.notes}
This will be the basis for Lab 12. Students will implement and test this.
:::

---

## 🤖 AI Prompting: Generics and Traits {.smaller}

**Design assistance:**
- "Help me design a generic cache data structure in Rust"
- "What traits should my custom type implement for common operations?"
- "Explain when to use trait bounds vs where clauses"

**Implementation help:**
- "How do I implement the Iterator trait for my custom struct?"
- "Why can't I return different types from a function returning impl Trait?"
- "Help me fix this trait bound error: [paste error]"

**Code review:**
- "Is this the idiomatic way to use generics in Rust?"
- "Should this be a generic function or use dynamic dispatch?"
- "How can I make these trait bounds more readable?"

::: {.notes}
AI is excellent for explaining trait system complexities and suggesting appropriate trait bounds.
:::

---

# Session 2: Traits Deep Dive

## Traits: Defining Shared Behavior {.inverse}

**What we'll cover:**

- Trait definitions and custom traits
- Implementing traits for your types
- Default implementations for code reuse
- Traits as function parameters
- Advanced trait bounds with where clauses
- Common standard library traits

**Goal**: Master Rust's powerful trait system for polymorphism and code reuse

::: {.notes}
Traits are one of Rust's most powerful features. They enable abstraction without inheritance.
:::

---

## Lab 12 Preview: Generic Stack {.inverse}

**What you'll build:**

- Generic `Stack<T>` data structure
- Implement core methods: `push`, `pop`, `peek`, `is_empty`, `len`
- Implement `Display` trait for custom formatting
- Implement `Iterator` trait to make your stack iterable
- Comprehensive test suite with 15+ tests

**Learning goals:**

- Apply generics to create reusable data structures
- Implement standard library traits
- Understand trait bounds in practice
- Write tests for generic types

**Deliverable**: A fully functional, well-tested generic stack!

::: {.notes}
This lab applies all the concepts from this week. Students will see how generics and traits work together in practice.
:::

---

## Career Relevance {.smaller}

**Why these skills matter:**

**Generics:**
- Foundation of modern language features (Java, C#, TypeScript, Swift, Go)
- Essential for library and framework development
- Interview questions: "Design a generic data structure"
- Understanding constraints and bounds

**Traits/Interfaces:**
- Interface design skills transfer to all languages
- Composition over inheritance (modern best practice)
- Critical for extensible systems and plugin architectures
- Type system understanding for advanced roles

**Real interview questions:**
- "Explain the difference between generics and dynamic dispatch"
- "Design an interface for [problem] - what methods would it have?"
- "How would you make this code reusable across different types?"
- "What are the tradeoffs between compile-time and runtime polymorphism?"

::: {.notes}
These skills are transferable across all modern programming languages. Employers value developers who understand abstraction mechanisms.
:::

---

## Key Takeaways {.inverse}

**Generics:**
- Enable code reuse without sacrificing type safety
- Zero-cost abstractions - no runtime overhead
- Work with structs, enums, functions, and methods
- Use trait bounds to constrain type parameters

**Traits:**
- Define shared behavior across types
- Enable polymorphism without inheritance
- Foundation of Rust's standard library
- Can have default implementations for convenience

**Together:**
- Generics + Traits = Powerful, reusable abstractions
- Type safety at compile time
- Expressive code that's still performant
- Industry-standard design patterns

::: {.notes}
These concepts work together to enable Rust's unique combination of performance and abstraction.
:::

---

## Additional Resources {.smaller}

**Official documentation:**
- [The Rust Book - Chapter 10: Generic Types, Traits, and Lifetimes](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch10-00-generics.html)
- [Rust by Example: Generics](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rust-by-example/generics.html)
- [Rust by Example: Traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/rust-by-example/trait.html)
- [Rust Standard Library Traits](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/#traits)

**Articles and guides:**
- [Rust Traits: A Deep Dive](https://oswalt.dev/2021/09/rust-traits-a-deep-dive/)
- [Generic Associated Types](https://blog.rust-lang.org/2022/10/28/gats-stabilization.html)
- [Tour of Rust's Standard Library Traits](https://github.com/pretzelhammer/rust-blog/blob/master/posts/tour-of-rusts-standard-library-traits.md)

**Tools:**
- [Rust Playground](https://play.rust-lang.org/) - Test code online
- [docs.rs](https://docs.rs/) - Browse crate documentation

::: {.notes}
Encourage students to explore these resources, especially the Tour of Rust's Standard Library Traits.
:::

---

## Next Week: Testing and Packaging {.inverse}

**Week 13 topics:**

- Test-driven development (TDD) workflow
- Unit testing vs integration testing
- Error handling with Result and Option
- Packaging Rust applications
- Building command-line tools

**Why it matters**: Learn to build complete, tested, distributable applications

**Focus**: Final project work begins - no new lab assignments

::: {.notes}
Week 13 covers testing and packaging so students can build complete applications for their final projects.
:::

---

## Questions? {.inverse}

**Get help:**

- Office hours: [Schedule on Teams](https://teams.microsoft.com)
- Course discussion: Microsoft Teams channel
- AI tools: Claude, ChatGPT, GitHub Copilot
- Rust community: [users.rust-lang.org](https://users.rust-lang.org)

**This week's materials:**

- [Week 12 Lecture Notes](../../resources/lecture-notes/W12_Generics_and_Traits_notes.md)
- [Week 12 Interactive Exercises](../../resources/lecture-notes/W12_Generics_and_Traits_exercises.md)
- [Lab 12: Generic Stack](../../week12/lab12.md)

---

## Start Lab 12! {.inverse}

**Lab due: Sunday, November 23, 2025, at 11:59 PM**

**What to do:**

1. Review lecture materials and examples
2. Work through interactive exercises
3. Begin Lab 12: Generic Stack implementation
4. Apply generics and traits from this week
5. Write comprehensive tests for your implementation
6. Ask questions early and often

**Remember**: You're not just learning Rust - you're learning universal programming concepts that apply everywhere!

**Good luck!** 🦀
